title: Cat Excessive Urination: Why Your Cat is Flooding the Litter Box
meta_desc: Is your cat flooding the litter box? Learn about polyuria, common causes like diabetes, and when to contact your vet for an appointment.
content_html:
What is Polyuria in Cats?

Excessive urination, medically referred to as polyuria, is a condition where a cat produces an abnormally large volume of urine. You may notice your cat spending more time in the litter box or finding unusually large, heavy clumps that seem to "flood" the litter. This is often a sign that the body is unable to concentrate urine properly or is trying to flush out toxins.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

The urgency level for excessive urination is generally Medium. While flooding the litter box is typically not an immediate life-threatening emergency like a urinary blockage (where a cat cannot urinate at all), it is a serious clinical sign of underlying illness. Conditions such as diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease, and hyperthyroidism are common culprits. You should aim to have your cat evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. If your cat is also lethargic, vomiting, or has a complete loss of appetite, contact an emergency clinic immediately.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a photo of the litter box can be incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. Seeing the size and frequency of the urine clumps provides a visual scale of the volume being produced, which is more precise than a verbal description. A photo of the urine's color or consistency on the litter can also help the vet differentiate between simple polyuria and other issues like hematuria (blood in the urine).

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of excessive urination?
faqs.answer: The most frequent causes in cats include feline diabetes, chronic kidney disease, hyperthyroidism, and occasionally urinary tract infections or liver issues.

faqs.question: Should I limit my cat's water if they are urinating too much?
faqs.answer: No, you should never restrict your cat's access to water. Increased urination usually causes dehydration, and your cat drinks more to compensate. Restricting water can lead to a dangerous medical crisis.

faqs.question: What tests will the vet perform?
faqs.answer: Your vet will likely recommend a urinalysis to check concentration and glucose levels, as well as blood work to evaluate kidney function, blood sugar, and thyroid levels.
